message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The Susceptible-Infectious-Recovered (
                  <jats:italic>SIR</jats:italic>
                  ) and
                  <jats:italic>SIR</jats:italic>
                  derived epidemic models have been commonly used to analyze the spread of infectious diseases. The underlying assumption in these models, such as Susceptible-Exposed-Infectious-Recovered (
                  <jats:italic>SEIR</jats:italic>
                  ) model, is that the change in variables
                  <jats:italic>E, I</jats:italic>
                  or
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  at time
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  is dependent on a fraction of
                  <jats:italic>E</jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                  at time
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  . This means that after exposed on a day, this individual may become contagious or even recover on the same day. However, the real situation is different: an exposed individual will become infectious after a latent period (
                  <jats:italic>l</jats:italic>
                  ) and then recover after an infectious period (
                  <jats:italic>i</jats:italic>
                  ). In this study, we proposed a new
                  <jats:italic>SEIR</jats:italic>
                  model based on the latent period-infectious period chronological order (Liu X., Results Phys. 2021; 20:103712). An analytical solution to equations of this new
                  <jats:italic>SEIR</jats:italic>
                  model was derived. From this new
                  <jats:italic>SEIR</jats:italic>
                  model, we obtained a propagated curve of infectious cases under conditions
                  <jats:italic>l</jats:italic>
                  >
                  <jats:italic>i</jats:italic>
                  . Similar propagated epidemic curves were reported in literature. However, the conventional
                  <jats:italic>SEIR</jats:italic>
                  model failed to simulate the propagated epidemic curves under the same conditions. For
                  <jats:italic>l</jats:italic>
                  <
                  <jats:italic>i</jats:italic>
                  , the new
                  <jats:italic>SEIR</jats:italic>
                  models generated bell-shaped curves for infectious cases, and the curve is near symmetrical to the vertical line passing the curve peak. This characteristic can be found in many epidemic curves of daily COVID-19 cases reported from different countries. However, the curve generated from the conventional
                  <jats:italic>SEIR</jats:italic>
                  model is a right-skewed bell-shaped curve. An example for applying the analytical solution of the new
                  <jats:italic>SEIR</jats:italic>
                  model equations to simulate the reported daily COVID-19 cases was also given in this paper.
                </jats:p>
title: Analytical Solution of a New SEIR Model Based on Latent Period-Infectious Period Chronological Order

reference.unstructured: Allen, L.J.S. , et al. Mathematical epidemiology, (Springer, 2008).

reference.article-title: A systematic review of studies on forecasting the dynamics of influenza outbreaks
A modified SEIR model to predict the COVID-19 outbreak in Spain and Italy: Simulating control scenarios and multi-scale epidemics

Weitz, J.S. , et al. Modeling shield immunity to reduce COVID-19 epidemic spread. Nature medicine (2020).

International journal of infectious diseases : IJID : official publication of the International Society for Infectious Diseases (2020).

reference.article-title: The effect of control strategies to reduce social mixing on outcomes of the COVID-19 epidemic in Wuhan, China: a modelling study

reference.unstructured: Giordano, G. , et al. Modelling the COVID-19 epidemic and implementation of population-wide interventions in Italy. Nature medicine (2020).
Chowdhury, R. , et al. Dynamic interventions to control COVID-19 pandemic: a multivariate prediction modelling study comparing 16 worldwide countries. European journal of epidemiology (2020).

reference.article-title: A simple, SIR-like but individual-based epidemic model: Application in comparison of COVID-19 in New York City and Wuhan
